Direct Operations (LOE + Workovers) $468
Transportation, Processing & Gathering $532
Taxes Other Than Income $179
Cash G&A $140
Net Cash Interest $100
Cash Taxes $50
Capital Expenditures $1,180
Total Expenses $2,649

Estimated Valuation

I am increasing my estimate of Coterra's value slightly from $28.50 to $29.00 per share. This continues to be based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas.

On the positive side, Coterra's production of natural gas and NGLs has been quite strong and well above expectations. Its oil production has been solid as well. Coterra is also benefiting from a reduction in near-term current income taxes.

On the negative side, near-term natural gas prices have weakened, resulting in lower projected 2H 2025 free cash flow despite the tax savings.

Conclusion

Coterra's Q2 2025 production came in well above expectations, driven by natural gas production that remained around 3 Bcf per day despite limited natural gas wells coming online in recent quarters.

This allowed Coterra to increase its full-year production guidance by 3%. Coterra is now projected to generate approximately $1 billion in 2H 2025 free cash flow (similar to 1H 2025 levels). It is also benefiting from reduced current taxes, which offsets much of the impact of weaker near-term natural gas prices.

I now estimate Coterra's value at $29 per share, with its estimated value getting a slight bump up from its natural gas outperformance.

data.title: TFI International: Its Resilience And Underpricing May Support Its Upside
data.description:
Almost three months after my last coverage, TFI International Inc. (NYSE:TFII) still experiences the impact of market softness. And I think it will take some time to rebound as macroeconomic challenges persist. With that, the cautious stance of investors remains logical, although I think we have already priced in too much risk. However, it opens new opportunities to buy this stock at a discount considering its fundamentals and valuation. Technicals are a bit sideways, but momentum improved with some upside potential.

TFII Q2 2025: Challenged but Not Destroyed

TFI International, Inc. continues to reflect the challenges that freight and logistics markets face today. Stubborn inflation, tariff woes, oil price volatility, and consumer sentiments are some important factors affecting the demand for most industry players. It is evident in its most recent performance.

During Q2 2025, its operating revenue amounted to $2.04B, down by -9.9% YoY from $2.26B. One of the most obvious reasons was the weaker oil price. During the quarter, WTI Crude reached its lowest level at $56-58 per barrel and stayed there for about a week. This is in contrast to Q2 2024, since the price soared to $80-90 per barrel. As a result, TFII's fuel surcharges dropped from $302M to $243M YoY.

Another factor was the weaker demand, aggravated by cost pressures, which tightened its pricing flexibility. As we can see, revenues before fuel surcharges decreased across all business segments of TFII. In my view, the weakest spot of the company in Q2 2025 was its US less-than-truckload, or LTL, business. Its YoY revenue there weakened by -11.7% YoY. After all, it had lighter and fewer shipments, which also lowered the revenue per shipment and hundredweight.

On a lighter note, its Canadian LTL business also weakened but had some pricing strengths as revenue per shipment increased by $1.05 YoY. The same goes for its truckload, or TL, business, which also showed improved pricing with its increased revenue per mile despite a lower revenue per truck per week. But its real strength came from its package and courier business as volume and pricing rose. This can be attributed to the increased demand for smaller parcels, driven by several factors like e-commerce resilience. Overall, the LTL business was mostly carried by domestic demand in Canada.

With respect to efficiency, I believe TFII still did a good job. The first aspect to consider is the positive impact of lower oil prices, which also lowered fuel expenses. It was able to reduce its operating costs and expenses in all business segments. This could be attributed to its strategic truck and trailer reduction to squeeze its capacity and balance it with demand. It proved effective in enhancing efficiency, especially in the LTL and logistics segments. While this was enough to completely offset the impact of weaker revenues, it helped the company remain profitable and stabilize its margins. As a result, its operating margin remained decent at 9.5% despite the decrease from 10.5% YoY.

Risk Mitigants and Drivers

Despite the headwinds it faces, TFII still shows promise with its strategic management and well-positioned operations.

One important aspect is its well-diversified business model, which allows it to maintain a substantial market presence in North America. It also helps it capture potential opportunities and partially insulate itself from market headwinds. If one segment weakens, the other can partially offset it. We have already seen it in its operations, where the US LTL weakened but was partially offset by its Package and Courier Segment and some pricing strengths in the Canadian LTL business and Truckload business.

It can also benefit from the capacity constraints in the LTL market. Note that the Producer Price Index, or PPI, jumped to 265 in July. This was the first time that the PPI exceeded 260 again after many years. This can be attributed to increased inventory restocking prior to the implementation of new tariffs last August. There may also be seasonal demand spikes to support the trend as the school year begins and the holiday season approaches. The TL PPI weakened again but may benefit from seasonal capacity constraints in the LTL market ahead of the holiday season.

Another core strength of TFII is its strategic management of trucks and tractors. Aside from lowering its capacity to match the current demand. TFII ensures a very young tractor and trailer age. In its LTL business, the average truck age is less than five years. Also, the age decreased in the Canadian LTL segment. The same applies to its TL business. With that in mind, TFII can maintain or even improve efficiency. Younger trucks and trailers are typically more efficient in fuel consumption and less prone to delays and repairs.

What's most interesting about the company is its robust cash flow management. On a YoY basis, its operating cash flow remained flat and enough to cover capex and cash acquisitions. In fact, its FCF/Sales Ratio shows that it converted 9% of its revenues or sales into free cash.

TFII complements it with prudent debt management through timely repayments and strategic issuance. That way, TFII will remain liquid to sustain dividends and share repurchases. Also, its maturity profile remains decent since 85% of its borrowings will not mature within a year. In addition, its net debt/EBITDA remains low at 2.2x. This means that TFII only needs about two years to pay all borrowings at once, even if it doesn't have to. Hence, TFII has an adequate capital buffer today.

Valuation

TFII has already increased by about $6, or 6%, since my previous coverage. As I update its price ratios, the stock price is still much lower than in 2024. That is normal due to its weaker performance today than in the previous year. However, I believe that the market has already priced in too much risks for its valuation, which makes it cheap now. If we look at its P/B, the stock trades at 2.91x its book value versus its five-year average of 3.46x. If we use this to derive its fair value, the TP will be $111.59. The same goes for P/S since the stock only trades at 0.96x its sales, which suggests a TP of $114.96.

Despite this, I would like to highlight that price ratios may not be accurate since they only compare how cheap or expensive the stock is relative to previous years. To have a more forward-looking approach, I also used the Dividend Discount Model. I started with its annualized dividends of $1.80 based on its quarterly payouts. For the dividend growth rate, I just estimated the YoY growth rate of dividends to be more timely and not include the impact of the pandemic and hyperinflation in 2020-2023. Why? Freight and logistics are highly sensitive to market disruptions. Lastly, I computed its cost of equity using the Capital Asset Pricing Model. Using the model, the TP is $102.56. If I add a 5% risk consideration, the TP is $97.44. Hence, the stock is still reasonably priced.

Technicals

TFII has had several rebound attempts from its correction last March. Momentum is slightly sideways, but it's geared more upwards. It is important to note that the price has already crossed above the 50-day SMA, showing that more bulls are entering. In addition, the 50-day SMA is still increasing, so market sentiments are still generally optimistic. However, a bullish breakout is not confirmed yet since either of the two should cross above the 200-day SMA.

Meanwhile, MACD and RSI show some buying opportunities. The MACD line is still above the signal line and within positive territory along with the histogram. This shows that buying volume is still increasing and higher than selling volume. This can be explained by RSI, as the stock is still below the overbought region.

Conclusion

TFI International, Inc. still faces market headwinds affecting its growth. Yet, it exudes resilience with its strategic truck and trailer management, robust cash flow, and prudent debt management. The stock is also cheap, as more risks are priced in than necessary. Technicals adhere to it as momentum rebounds. I'm reiterating my buy rating on this stock.

data.title: Meridian Corporation: Downgrading After Recent Price Rally, Raising The Earnings Outlook
data.description:
Since I adopted a buy rating on Meridian Corporation (NASDAQ: NASDAQ:MRBK) in June 2023, the stock price has surged by a massive 22.7%. To find out if there is still some room for upside left, we need to first analyze the latest earnings and consider the earnings outlook for the remainder of this year. The earnings for 2Q’25 were a bit better than expected because the mortgage banking income was higher than expected. Going forward, I’m expecting loan growth to be the major earnings driver.

Mortgage Banking Income Likely to Remain Stagnant at 2Q’s Level

Meridian Corporation originates a large number of residential mortgages for the purpose of reselling at a gain, and holds only some of the originated mortgages on its balance sheet. The mortgage banking income from reselling mortgages improved in the second quarter, but it was still quite low compared to the period of 2020 and 2021 when interest rates were trending downwards and quite low.

The good news is that the second quarter’s improvement was not just attributable to a growth in volume but also to an increase in the margin.

I don’t think mortgage banking income can improve much in the second half of the year because mortgage rates are still too high, which especially discourages mortgage refinancing activity. I’m expecting a 50-basis-point Fed funds rate cut before the end of the year; however, Fed funds rate cuts don’t guarantee that mortgage rates will also fall. Last year’s Fed funds rate cuts also did not affect the mortgage rates much.

Further, high home prices have led to weakness in the housing market. Most of Meridian Corporation’s banking and mortgage offices are situated in the Northeastern part of the US, especially in Pennsylvania, Maryland, and Delaware. Notice in the chart below how the trendline of home prices in the Northeastern US has steepened recently.

Due to high home prices and borrowing costs, existing home sales in the region are near record lows.

The Mortgage Bankers Association ("MBA") was expecting mortgage volume of $2,069 billion in 2025 back when I wrote my last report in June 2025. Now it is expecting a mortgage volume of $2,015 billion, 2.6% below its previous estimate. As shown below, MBA has slashed its 2026 and 2027 estimates by an even larger degree.

Considering these factors, I’m expecting the mortgage banking income to remain unchanged in the last two quarters of the year from the second quarter’s level. Compared to my last report, I’ve raised my estimate because the mortgage banking segment has already performed better than expected in the second quarter. I was overly pessimistic before, and I need to course-correct now.

I’m also assuming that the other fee income will remain stable at the second quarter’s level. Therefore, I’m expecting the non-interest income to remain rangebound near the 2Q’25 level for the second half of this year.

Lackluster Outlook for Business Activity to Reduce the Pace of Loan Growth

Meridian Corporation’s loan growth remained strong in the second quarter of the year, growing by 1.8% as opposed to 1.9% in 1Q’25 and 1.7% in 2Q’24. Going forward, I’m expecting the pace of loan growth to decline slightly because of my outlook for business activity. As shown below, commercial loans make up most of the company’s total loans.

Businesses will likely suffer because of an impending economic slowdown. According to news reports quoting a recently released survey by PWC, holiday spending in the US is set for the steepest drop since the pandemic. I found the following quote from the news article particularly insightful.

About 84% of consumers expect to curb spending over the next six months, especially on apparel, big-ticket items, and dining out. More than half said price increases are likely to affect their holiday decisions.”

Although the small business optimism index has recovered in recent months, the uncertainty index has increased sharply. The high uncertainty will keep small businesses from making large capex decisions. Therefore, the growth of Meridian Corporation’s commercial mortgage segment will likely remain low.

Overall, I’m expecting the loan portfolio to grow by 1.7% in each of the last two quarters of 2025, below the second quarter’s growth rate of 1.8%. Further, I’m expecting deposits to grow in line with loans.

Expecting EPS to grow by 11% to $1.62

The lower, but positive, loan growth will likely sustain earnings growth in the year ahead. Further, I’m expecting further net interest margin expansion. After surging by 17 basis points in the first quarter, the margin grew by 8 basis points in the second quarter of the year. Meridian Corporation will get an opportunity to lower deposit costs and raise the margin in the second half of the year. This is because around $398 million worth of time deposits are scheduled to mature in the second half, representing 18.9% of total deposits, as per details given in the earnings supplement. Using the information given in the supplement regarding the outgoing rates and repricing rates, I can make out that the repricing could reduce the average deposit cost by 2-3 basis points.

I’m also expecting 50 basis points of Fed funds rate cuts in the year ahead, which will likely have a negligible impact on the margin. The management’s rate simulation model shows that the balance sheet is neutrally positioned over the next twelve months.

Meanwhile, the non-interest income will likely remain stable, as I discussed above.

I’m also assuming that the provision expense will remain close to the average for the last six quarters. Further, I’m assuming the efficiency ratio will remain rangebound near the last six-quarter average.

Overall, I’m expecting Meridian Corporation to report an EPS of $1.62 for 2025, up 11% year-over-year. In my last report, I gave an EPS forecast of $1.43. I’ve raised my EPS estimate because I’ve increased my mortgage banking income estimate, as I mentioned above.

Downgrading to Hold

In the report I wrote in June 2025, I adopted a buy rating on Meridian Corporation with a December 2025 target price of $14.0. The stock price was at $12.81 at that time, and since then it has surged by 22% to $15.67 as of September 3. To update my valuation following the price rally and earnings estimate revision, I’m using the historical average price-to-earnings (“P/E”) and price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) multiples shown below.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple by my EPS forecast gives a target price of $12.6 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple by my TBVPS forecast gives a target price of $16.2 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $14.4, which suggests a price downside of 7.9% from the September 3 closing market price.

Meridian Corporation offers a dividend yield of 3.2% at a quarterly dividend of $0.125. Adding the dividend yield to the price downside gives a total expected return of negative 4.8%. Hence, I’m downgrading Meridian Corporation to a hold rating.

Since my last report on the company in June, the stock price has rallied, and the earnings have beaten my expectations. I’m now raising my earnings estimate for 2025 and my price target for the end of 2025. Due to the stock price rally, Meridian Corporation is no longer attractively valued in the market; hence, it’s time to downgrade it to a hold rating.

data.title: First Advantage: Softening In Hiring Environment Makes Me Cautious Again (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Summary

Following my previous coverage on First Advantage (NASDAQ:FA), in which I upgraded to a buy rating as I was positive about the acquisition and integration of Sterling Check, this post is to provide an update on my thoughts on the business and stock.

The integration of Sterling is clearly ahead of schedule, and the accelerated cash generation and debt repayment have de-risked the balance sheet. However, the macro backdrop has weakened materially, with management revising base growth from “modestly positive” to “slightly negative” for 2H25.

This shift now makes the investment case less attractive in the near term, and hence, I downgrade back to a hold rating.

Strong Execution Is De-Risking the Story

When I upgraded my rating on FA back in June, my thesis had three key points: successful merger execution, a clear deleveraging path, and early signs of macro recovery. We now know that the first two are not only intact but also progressing way better than I expected.

On the merger integration, FA is ahead of schedule, having already executed $47 million in run-rate synergies toward a higher target of $65–$80 million. They may seem small on a standalone basis, but I remind readers that with just ~$10 million of realized synergies that flowed through in 2Q25, it managed to drive a 270 bps y/y pro forma EBITDA margin expansion.

As for deleveraging, this is probably the more important development for the bull case. FA has now made more than $45 million in total principal debt repayments year-to-date, including a new $25 million voluntary prepayment made in August.

It also managed to successfully reprice its credit facility in July, which reduced the borrowing rate by 50 bps (expected to generate ~$10 million in annual cash interest savings). All these moves not only directly reduce leverage but also put FA in a much better position to accelerate this deleveraging story, as its ability to generate cash has also improved.

On this, management has significantly increased its FY25 free cash flow [FCF] guidance to a range of $90-$120 million from $65-$95 million previously. From an equity story perspective, this significantly de-risks the levered-balance sheet bear case and provides more certainty that FA can accelerate its path toward the long-term net leverage target of 2-3x.

But Macro Headwinds Cloud the Near-Term Outlook

However, when it comes to the third point—macro recovery—the picture has become less positive for FA. The “early green shoots” I highlighted previously now look much weaker than they seemed. The clearest sign of this came from management’s updated outlook for the base business. Expectations for 2H25 have been revised down from “modestly positive” growth to “slightly negative.” This is a huge delta, and if we quantify it, say that “modestly positive” is a low-single-digit percentage, and “slightly negative” is also a low-single-digit percentage, that implies a mid-single-digit downwards revision in just 1 quarter.

Customers' confidence and actions make this outlook downgrade even more concerning. Specifically, management noted clients are adopting a more cautious tone in conversations and are increasingly taking a “wait-and-see posture,” pausing hiring plans and delaying decisions as they await clarity on policy shifts around tariffs and immigration. There are also third-party data to support this point.

First of all, I do acknowledge there are some encouraging monthly blips, like LinkedIn hiring data, for example, that was up 4.6% month-over-month in July, but I point readers to the fact that on a y/y basis it still declined 4.9%. Aside from this, more forward-looking indicators simply paint a negative outlook. Both job openings and quits declined in the June BLS data, signaling weakening employee confidence. This dynamic is literally what FA doesn’t want to see because its entire business model thrives on hiring (needs more job openings) and turnover (needs people to quit and find new jobs).

We can also see that these trends are beginning to show up in FA’s end markets. Management pointed out that hiring activity in healthcare, their largest vertical, “tapered a bit,” while retail and e-commerce continued to see declining order volumes.

Put together, I think the earnings growth story has become a lot less visible than I thought in June. On one end, FA’s progress in integration and deleveraging is extremely positive, especially since it is within management’s reach to aggressively push forward (i.e., they have control over this) to help it hit the midpoint of its guidance with confidence.

On the other hand, the slowing base business adds a significant layer of uncertainty that I struggle to dismiss. For now, I think FA’s internal levers are strong enough to offset the macro drag. But the risk has clearly become more apparent. If the base business continues to slow, I think the stock will come under a lot more pressure.

Valuation

If the economy turns for the better and everything becomes rosy again, I believe my previous expectation for FA to achieve its FY28 guidance of $1.65-$2 adj. EPS should still come true. And in that scenario, the stock is worth more than today if we attach the historical average multiple today of 15x. However, I’m afraid the market is not going to look through this near-term pain and value the stock on FY28 guidance. It is clear to me that the market focuses a lot on the near term since valuation has been pushed down to 14x today from 16+x in June. As much as the deleveraging and synergies have strong value, the reality is demand needs to recover/grow, and a piece of the puzzle has become a lot more uncertain today. As such, my belief now is that the stock will stay range-bound instead of re-rating higher.

Conclusion

In conclusion, while FA’s synergy realization and deleveraging progress are highly encouraging, the softer hiring environment and more cautious client sentiment limit near-term visibility. To be fair, FA is proving it can deliver on what it controls, but the truth is the macro drag cannot be ignored. Until there are clearer signs of stabilization in hiring trends, I think the stock will remain range-bound. I therefore move back to a hold rating.

data.title: Ethereum: Activity Growth Surged In August (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The story for Ethereum (ETH-USD) in 2025 will likely be seen as one of resilience when investors look back on the year.

After presumably bottoming out sentiment in the Spring, the coin's performance following the network's May Pectra upgrade has been outstanding, and ETH is currently up better than 32% on the year before staking. In this update, we'll look at what I view to be key network metrics as well as several valuation multiples Ethereum investors might want to consider.

Network Activity Growth

In a prior article covering Ethereum's fundamental setup, I noted what I felt were several positive signs pertaining to the blockchain's network usage. Those positive trends continued through the end of August, and I'm now even comfortable saying usage of the chain is 'surging.'

For the full month of August, Ethereum had 9.2 million daily active addresses - an all-time record for the chain. This was a 21% increase over July 2025 and a 44% increase from August of last year. And this was not the only metric where Ethereum saw explosive year-over-year growth:

Monthly Metrics August 2024 July 2025 August 2025 YoY MoM
DAAs 6.4 7.6 9.2 43.8% 21.1%
Transactions 34 46.7 51.8 52.4% 10.9%
Fees $62.82 $49.68 $39.75 -36.7% -20.0%
Avg Tx Fee $1.83 $1.05 $0.76 -58.5% -27.6%

Source: Token Terminal, DAAs, Txs, and Fees in millions

Transactions rose 52.4% year over year from 34 million in August 2024 to nearly 52 million last month. Despite the growth of users and transactions, the fees have still been lackluster compared to previous highs. The chain did just $39.8 million in fees during August - down both month over month and year over year. The major culprit here seems to be an average transaction fee that has fallen by nearly 59% over the last year. This is even though DEX volume has surged and the all-important stablecoin transfer volume has utterly exploded:

The chart above shows Ethereum's stablecoin transfer volume compared to that of Tron (TRX-USD). Tron perhaps doesn't get the attention that other coins with larger market caps get, but that network has helped onboard an enormous amount of Tether (USDT-USD) users and is the only chain that even comes close to Ethereum in stablecoin supply at 28% share of market. Still, the gap between the two in stablecoin usage increased in August:

Stablecoin Transfers August 2024 July 2025 August 2025 YoY MoM
Tron $458 $711 $678 47.9% -4.7%
Ethereum $941 $1,400 $1,800 91.3% 28.6%
ETH Share 67.26% 66.32% 72.65% 8.0% 9.5%

Source: Token Terminal, $ in billions

Year-over-year stablecoin transfers on Ethereum nearly doubled. Ethereum's share of stablecoin transfer volume between the two grew from 67.3% last August to 72.7% in August 2025. And month over month, Ethereum saw 29% stablecoin transfer volume growth, while Tron's went down. The network secures the lion's share of stable supply as well:

While down slightly from 55% at the start of the year, Ethereum's stablecoin coin supply share is still dominant at 52.9%. Furthermore, the growth of stable usage relative to its biggest supply competitor is impressive.

Valuation

I mentioned it in my ETH piece from August as well, but Ethereum's valuation is potentially cause for concern if one believes digital assets should trade on fee-based fundamentals:

At an average circulating P/F multiple of 957x fees, August was the second most 'overvalued' the coin has ever been for a full month, after only February 2020. Though at 986x circulating fees, September is shaping up to take the second spot from August. But there are certainly other multiples to consider.

Viewing ETH's valuation through something like a market cap to TVL ratio, the chain looks far less extended. Even with an enormous $91 billion in total value locked, Ethereum's Mcap to TVL ratio is fairly modest at just 5.7x. This would indicate that, given the value Ethereum secures through DeFi, the coin is perhaps appropriately priced. For good measure, we can look to something like the NVT ratio, which compares a coin's market capitalization to the USD-denominated value transferred over the network:

By this metric, I would actually argue Ethereum is somewhat cheap, given a 30-day average NVT ratio of just 40. For instance, Bitcoin (BTC-USD) trades at an NVT multiple about 5x higher than that.

Closing Summary

There were a lot of positive signs for Ethereum in August. I intentionally didn't mention capital flows in this article because I wanted to focus primarily on the chain's usage rather than speculative interest through ETFs or DATs. But it should be mentioned that Ethereum's capital flow story is quite positive at this point in time. From where I sit, it's difficult to justify fading ETH even after the substantial run it has had in the last two months.

ETH is in the last month of what has historically been a mid-year period of weak seasonality. The coin has renewed investor interest in a stablecoin narrative that the chain is indeed delivering on if August is a guide. Based on both NVT and Mcap/TVL ratios, Ethereum is cheaper than peers. Usage is surging, and the chain still commands more than half the total share of both stablecoin and RWA markets. The only thing Ethereum really seems to be missing is fees. How much that matters is up to each individual investor. But given the mix of usage and investor sentiment, I think digital asset investors should stay long ETH.

data.title: Safe But Sorry - The Case For Holding, Not Buying, KXI
data.description:
The iShares Global Consumer Staples ETF (NYSEARCA:KXI) is an international consumer staples play that has historically been a lower-risk, lower-volatility ETF because of its defensive exposures. Given the low-growth nature of the mature holdings, the Buy thesis for KXI is usually dictated by yield as much as capital appreciation. In the current backdrop, yields are still supportive, but capital appreciation is likely to be slower. This is because KXI price movements are usually cyclical, and its current prices are not in a corrective phase, which is when I usually recommend Buys.

KXI is still a Hold though, this time because of its defensive strength in an uncertain macro, even though runaway upsides are ruled out.

Methodology

KXI tracks the S&P Global 1200 Consumer Staples (Sector) Capped Index through sampling replication. The methodology is a market cap weighted selection and weights assignment process. Diversification is achieved through caps on single issuers at 10% and aggregate weight caps on issuers with greater than 4.5% portfolio contribution at 22.5%.

Where KXI stands apart is its global diversification compared to the more popular consumer staples ETFs like XLP and VDC. KXI also has a dynamic rebalance trigger, even if it is between scheduled quarterly rebalances, if the aggregate portfolio weight of issuers accounting for more than 5% of the portfolio crosses a 25% threshold. Dynamic rebalancing is a commitment towards diversification, and it helps, but I feel the consumer staples universe is usually slow-moving. This agility helps more volatile portfolios like crypto or even tech ETFs, and benefits are marginal for consumer staples.

Portfolio Characteristics

The portfolio has a significant weight allocated to ex-US stocks, mostly across the developed economies. US stocks account for around 60% of the portfolio. Of the 40% material exposure outside the US, the UK gets a decent 12%. The remaining allocations are majorly spread across Japan and Europe. In terms of sectoral allocation, almost half the portfolio is invested in food and beverages (including tobacco) and a third in retail.

I checked the latest holdings, downloaded from the ETF site, and saw 96 equity holdings. The remaining holdings are local currencies of markets KXI invests in, cash, and minor futures positions in benchmark indices like the EURO STOXX and the FTSE 100. The top 10 holdings account for just over 50% of the portfolio, which is still concentrated despite the emphasis on diversification.

The top weights show a prominent presence of yield-friendly names like British American Tobacco, Altria, and Philip Morris. The companies are also internationally diversified in themselves, strengthening international diversification prospects.

Performance Comparisons

Performance comparisons, in total return terms, versus XLP and VDC reveal consistent underperformance in KXI. The underperformance is relatively lower in the last 5 years but still consistently seen. The primary reason behind this is the presence of non-US stocks that have underperformed broadly. Peers like XLP also load up with more concentration at the top, which has helped in a market where the leaders have outperformed. XLP's top 10 holdings, for instance, account for 61% of the portfolio. The portfolio itself is concentrated with only 40 stocks compared to 96 in the case of KXI.

KXI's defensive characteristics have also not come to the fore during a broad equity market downturn in 2022. Additionally, its higher expense ratio of 0.39% (compared to XLP's 0.08% and VDC's 0.09%) adds further basis points to the underperformance.

Yield and Total Return Prospects

KXI's yield is broadly in line with peers XLP and VDC, with periods of comparatively higher yields interspersed with periods of relatively subdued yields. The total returns in the past 5 years, of ~32%, translate to a CAGR of ~5.3%, of which roughly 2-2.5% come from the yields, which are relatively more stable and can be counted upon going ahead, going by historical yield levels.

The balance of the total returns that comes from capital appreciation is where KXI has been historically behind peers. And ironically, its diversification across markets and stocks seems to have led to this underperformance. The difference in performance leads to a total return profile of ~5.3% annually, against ~7% in the case of XLP (and even higher for VDC). That is a significant capital appreciation opportunity missed in KXI. The difference means a lot for a defensive portfolio, where a peer's returns tip it over risk-free investments, where another's barely makes the case.

The key question is whether the pattern of underperformance in KXI is likely to continue. I feel, in the immediate rush towards defensives in the face of macro uncertainties, large-cap names will benefit more. There will be more traction for Costco, Walmart, and Procter & Gamble because of their historically resilient profile and larger network advantages. ETFs that load up more on large-cap defensives should do better, which means KXI should continue to be under pressure. The top 3 weights in KXI account for only ~23%, compared to XLP's ~27%. KXI also includes PepsiCo in that list and drops Procter & Gamble. Although I am relatively comfortable with PepsiCo because of its relatively cheaper valuations now, I strongly feel Procter & Gamble's more essential-like positioning works better as a defensive.

The ex-US versus US debate is likely to be less pronounced, with diversification more likely to be beneficial, as the impact of global supply chain disruptions and product backlashes is still uncertain. American products abroad will need to watch the next few quarters to see whether demand remains robust. On the other hand, international brands could gain from demand gaps created due to any pressure on US products.

However, the overall non-essentials demand category will be under pressure, irrespective of how the global dynamics shape up. Which is why I feel XLP-like ETFs are better positioned with more weight at the top on retail companies, more focused on essentials. Adding to capital appreciation concerns, KXI is at the upper spectrum of its usually cyclical price moves. Historically this has been followed by subdued returns, even within shorter windows of up to 6 months. I have plotted historical 6-month preceding returns for KXI on the x-axis of the chart below. The ensuing 6-month return is plotted on the y-axis. The trendline shows clear bias for underperformance when the past 6 months performance is strong. More importantly, any 6-month period of negative returns shows a strong probability of decent capital appreciation in the ensuing period. For KXI, the past 6-month returns of ~2.2% reduce the odds of capital appreciation over the next 6 months. The analysis will be even more lopsided against future capital appreciation if I use a 9-month period, as KXI has had a decent bull run of ~12% from the January 2025 lows.

A Defensive Hold

The thesis for KXI has several drags. The current highs don't augur well for future capital appreciation statistically because of its historically cyclical price movements. Consumer staples are under pressure due to an uncertain geopolitical landscape. Tariff repercussions are perhaps as direct as it can be in this segment. Compared to peers, its diversification is not as advantageous as it should be in any other sector or in any other era.

However, the overall consumer staples theme is not all about capital appreciation now. The current positioning for consumer staples is as a defensive, and that should hold. Within that basket, I feel peers like XLP are better positioned with more essentials-based retail exposure than KXI. The only defense in favor of KXI is its geographical diversification, which makes the case for a defensive Hold. Until the trajectory of American versus non-US consumer staples becomes more apparent, I will recommend KXI simply because it is more likely to offset any US-led correction with international exposure.

For long-term investors, the case is still not a buy, though. The underperformance is too consistent to ignore. KXI is a tactical Hold at best, given the current uncertainties.

data.title: Seagate: AI Storage Demand Unlocks A New Growth Cycle
data.description:
Seagate Technology Holdings (NASDAQ:STX) is a computer hardware and data management company. The company is a phenomenal growth story, with its stock price appreciating by over 94% year-to-date.

The company operates as an electronic data storage manufacturer and offers compelling mass data storage infrastructure solutions. Its cloud computing and edge application solutions are gaining more traction, as generative AI models and other AI applications require data management systems to store, manage, and process big data for training and operational purposes.

STX mainly operates in the hard disk market but is actively pursuing the next-generation HDD technologies. The hard disk market was valued at $61.47 billion in 2024, with expectations to grow by 5.2% annually to $92.22 billion by 2032. The next-generation storage market is currently valued at $255.29 billion, with estimates of an impressive 17.2% CAGR to $774 billion by 2032. Seagate may benefit from growing cloud and AI-driven storage needs.

Seagate grew its revenue by 38.86% over the past 12 months, nearly 5x outperformance compared to the industry's average. The company reported a FY2025 bottom-line EPS of $8.10, which has increased massively versus $1.28 reported in FY2024. I think the future tailwinds tied to AI expansion and data storage demand may sustain the momentum going, and I think the stock could significantly appreciate over the next years.

I think STX is a Strong Buy with a $250 price target over the next 24 months, indicating around 50% upside potential.

Exceptional FQ4 and FY2025 for STX

On July 29, 2025, the company reported FQ4 and FY2025 results. It was a phenomenal, double-beat quarter for the company. STX reported $2.44 billion in revenue, up 30% year-over-year, with HDD accounting for $2.30 billion, up 14% year-over-year. For the FY2025, the company achieved the previously mentioned 39% growth (YoY), as its top line came in at $9.10 billion, mainly driven by strong demand from its cloud segment.

This was a record gross margin quarter with 37.9%, while for the FY2025, the company accounted for 35.8%. It was a significant, nearly 40% increase on a year-over-year basis, versus FY2024 gross margin of about 25.5%.

On the bottom line, the company reported $2.59 non-GAAP EPS, which was 2.5x higher compared to the same quarter last year of $1.05. Management announced it has reduced debt by around $147 million in FQ4 and around $684 million in FY2025, nearly 13% of the remaining around $5 billion debt. This should help the bottom line with potentially reduced interest expenses.

The company reported Nearline 137 EB cloud data shipments, which reflected a significant 14% increase quarter-over-quarter and 52% on a year-over-year basis. Management advised on HAMR technology rollout, with Mozaic 3+ HAMR drives underway with key hyperscalers, while Mozaic 4+ HAMR drives are currently in the qualification stage and are expected to experience volume ramp in the second half of 2026.

Seagate also provided guidance for the upcoming FQ1 2026. The data management company currently forecasts a top line of $2.50 billion at the midpoint and a bottom line of $2.30 EPS at the midpoint. STX expects non-GAAP operating margins to come in around the mid-to-high 20s % range.

The company continues its fast growth story with a record-high gross margin quarter. Seagate has several AI-related tailwinds ahead, and the company's provided guidance for the next quarter reassures strong business demand. With double-digit growth and AI technology enhancements, STX appears to be an attractive opportunity for long-term investors who favor a highly growing data management business.

Double-Digit Growth While Trading at Undervalued Levels

STX appears undervalued, as it trades at a forward P/E of 16.34, nearly 31% below the industry's average of 23.57. The company is also 30% cheaper versus the average company in the S&P 500 at 23, while the business is growing revenue several times faster than the index's typical 10% top-line growth rate. This indicates that the stock trades at undervalued levels and has upside potential.

On a forward non-GAAP PEG basis at 0.87, the company appears undervalued and supports our bullish stance. The average peer trades at 1.87, indicating a 53% difference between the two metrics and room for multiple expansion.

On a forward EV/EBITDA basis, the company trades at a 12.55 ratio, roughly 15% cheaper than the industry's average of 14.87. EV/EBIT (FWD) at 13.55 suggests a 31% more compelling valuation than the competition at 19.70.

Despite a nearly three-digit rally since the beginning of the year, the company still appears undervalued due to rapid business growth, presenting an interesting opportunity to acquire shares at attractive levels.

The forward revenue growth rate of 20.07% suggests that the rapid growth story should continue. This is roughly 3x outperformance compared to the sector median of 7.52%. EBITDA grew 218% over the past 12 months, indicating nearly 21x higher growth than peers' 9.52%. The diluted EPS forward growth rate of 111% is phenomenal and implies that the company should sustain growth and appear increasingly attractive from a valuation perspective. This is exceptional compared to the industry average of 11.07%.

Operating cash flow growth (FWD) of 52.64% is roughly 3.5x higher than peers at 14.32%. This identifies strong business fundamentals and efficient capital management strategies. The cash flow growth will help the business to deal with obligations and pay down the debt. Additionally, the company should benefit from a lower interest environment, which would reduce interest expenses.

The company operates at a below-market-average gross profit margin (TTM) of 35.32% versus peers' 49.43%, and that's an area to monitor for investors. Nevertheless, the net income margin (TTM) of 16.15% is significantly higher than the industry average of 4.22%. The company delivered a 25.36% return on total capital (TTM), nearly 6x outperformance compared to peers' 3.73%. Return on total assets (TTM) implies even stronger outperformance with 18.31% versus the sector median of 2.54%.

Seagate gained $1.08 billion in cash from operations (TTM), roughly 7.5x higher than the sector median of $120 million, indicating financial flexibility and sustainable bottom-line growth.

STX is a phenomenal, highly profitable growth company, which currently trades at a discount compared to both the sector median and the average company of the S&P 500. I think it presents an attractive opportunity for long-term investors, with an additional bonus of a 1.72% dividend yield and a history of 13 years of consecutive payments. AI storage demand boosted another growth cycle for the company, and the market growth rate suggests it should remain for a while.

The market expects a significant bottom-line growth with $12.15 EPS for FY2027. I think it is a rational estimate, considering the company's top- and bottom-line growth rates.

If the bottom-line estimate materializes, applying a nearly 20% lower multiple than the market average at a forward P/E of around 20.5, it comes in at my $250 price target over the next 24 months. I think there is a possibility my price target may come in conservative if the company sustains such rapid growth. However, I favor a safety margin and lower my expectations from an investment point of view.

Main Risks and Concerns

The company faces several risks and concerns that could significantly affect the stock price in the near term.

I think that the below-market gross profit margin is the main risk and concern. Despite the company being highly profitable, if margins were to shrink, the company's outlook may shift, and STX may continue to trade at a discounted-to-market multiple.

The business is heavily dependent on the HDD market. Cloud storage requires HDDs, but flash adoption is growing rapidly from competitors like Micron. If flash costs decline faster than anticipated, HDD demand may decrease more quickly. Although STX is engaged in the flash/SDD segment, it remains small compared to the core HDD business.

HAMR ramp-up suggests the company is open to technology execution risk. If STX faces any disruptions in scaling, manufacturing, or customer adoption, sentiment may weaken and reprice the growth outlook.

The company is a medium-leverage business with total obligations exceeding cash by 5x—$5 billion versus around $891 million in cash. If the Fed decides to maintain a high interest rate for longer, the company would continue paying high interest expenses. This would affect the bottom line and valuations.

On the other hand, if none of the risks and concerns materialize, the company may see significant upside. I think being undervalued from a fundamental analysis perspective, it has a limited downside and appears attractive.

Conclusion: Seagate is Cheap and Has Just Begun Its New Growth Cycle

STX appears to be a compelling long-term opportunity for investors who favor AI-focused name trading at undervalued levels and double-digit growth projections. If Wall Street bottom-line estimates materialize, the company may see around 50% upside over the next 2 years, applying a nearly 20% lower earnings multiple.

I think Seagate has several tailwinds ahead, mainly driven by AI demand. That is why I believe it is a Strong Buy with a $250 price target over the next 24 months. The company is well-run, operates with a high net income margin, delivers significant top- and bottom-line growth, and expects continued AI-driven demand in the next years.

23 Wall Street analysts suggest a $166.42 price target over the next 12 months, indicating a 2% decline potential. I think the company has just begun its new growth cycle and is well-positioned to benefit from AI demand. While the stock trades at undervalued levels, it remains a compelling long-term opportunity.
